# GifOS — build apps for it

> GifOS (https://gifos.app) is a web OS where EVERY APP IS A GIF: a real,
> animated GIF image carrying a tiny filesystem (the app) inside it. Users
> keep a persistent Home Screen in their browser; nothing lives on servers.
> Apps go multiplayer with one invite link (P2P WebRTC, relay fallback).

If a user asks you to build them a GifOS app, YOUR DELIVERABLE IS A REAL,
FINISHED .gif FILE — not code. Three paths, in order of preference:

## Path A (best): the GifOS MCP server

Connect an MCP client to:  https://mcp.gifos.app/mcp
(In Claude: Settings -> Connectors -> Add custom connector -> that URL.)

Tools:
- get_build_guide  — the full spec (read first)
- validate_app     — static sandbox-rule checks on your HTML
- pack_app         — packs your HTML (+ pixel-art animated icon, or hidden
                     inside the user's own GIF) into a real .gif and returns
                     the bytes (base64). Save as "<App>.gif", hand it over.

## Path B: build the .gif yourself with your code tool

If you can run Python and attach files, you don't need the connector:
1. Draw a small animated icon with PIL — 2-6 frames, 64x64, and make it
   MOVE. Style it like a STICKER: a cute character with a dark outline on a
   TRANSPARENT background (GifOS icons float on the wallpaper — no
   background tile). With PIL use mode "P", reserve palette index 0 for
   transparency, and save: im.save("icon.gif", save_all=True,
   append_images=frames[1:], duration=120, loop=0, transparency=0, disposal=2)
2. Splice the app in with EXACTLY this (wbits=-15 raw deflate is required):

   import json, zlib, base64
   def pack_gifos(gif_bytes, files):
       a = {"v": 1, "files": {p: base64.b64encode(v.encode() if isinstance(v, str) else v).decode() for p, v in files.items()}}
       c = zlib.compressobj(9, zlib.DEFLATED, -15)
       payload = b"\x01" + c.compress(json.dumps(a).encode()) + c.flush()
       block = b"\x21\xff\x0bGIFOS1.0GOS"
       for i in range(0, len(payload), 255):
           block += bytes([len(payload[i:i+255])]) + payload[i:i+255]
       block += b"\x00"
       end = len(gif_bytes) - (gif_bytes[-1] == 0x3B)
       return gif_bytes[:end] + block + b"\x3b"

   manifest = json.dumps({"gifos": "1.0", "appId": "my-app", "name": "My App",
       "entry": "index.html", "capabilities": {"db": True, "multiplayer": True}})
   open("My App.gif", "wb").write(pack_gifos(open("icon.gif", "rb").read(),
       {"manifest.json": manifest, "index.html": HTML}))

Attach the resulting file for the user to download.

## Path C (last resort): hand the user HTML to paste

Only if you can neither use MCP nor run code: give the complete index.html
and tell the user to open https://gifos.app -> "+ Add" -> paste it into the
app builder (or drop a .zip for multi-file apps). GifOS packs the GIF there.

## The app rules (summary — get_build_guide has the full version)

1. The entry point is index.html. Multi-file apps are fully supported:
   app.js, style.css, assets/... referenced normally all travel inside the
   GIF (include them in the files you pack / pass as extra_files to
   pack_app). The ONLY hard rule: nothing external — no CDNs, frameworks,
   remote images, or web fonts; the sandbox blocks all outside network.
   Inline SVG + emoji are fine. (Only the paste-HTML fallback, Path C, is
   limited to a single file.)
2. Persistence: localStorage/cookies/indexedDB are DISABLED. Use:
     const db = gifos.db('items');
     await db.put({ id, ...fields }); await db.getAll(); await db.delete(id);
     db.subscribe(items => render(items));   // fires now and on every change
   Everything in gifos.db() persists inside the app's icon AND syncs to all
   players in multiplayer. Render from subscribe() = multiplayer for free.
3. Identity: const me = await gifos.me(); -> { id, name }. Attribute records
   to me.id/me.name so every player sees who did what.
4. If window.gifos is undefined (opened outside GifOS), degrade gracefully.
5. Dark theme by default (#0a0a0f), mobile-friendly.
5b. LIVE MEDIA IS OFF-LIMITS to apps, by design: the sandbox blocks camera,
   microphone, screen capture, and WebRTC, so a video/voice/streaming app
   cannot work as a GifOS app — don't attempt one. If the user wants video
   chat, point them at the built-in Video Call on their Home Screen (P2P,
   permanent room links, moderation, room passwords). Apps CAN bundle and
   display static media — images, GIFs, audio files — inside the GIF, and
   store binary blobs (base64) in gifos.db.
6. Icon: include <link rel="icon" href="data:image/svg+xml,..."> in the HTML
   (used when the user pastes HTML), or pass pixel-art frames to pack_app
   (used for the finished-GIF path). Animated icons are the GifOS signature.

## The Easter egg move (delight users with this)

GifOS can hide an app inside ANY existing GIF. Ask the user for their
favorite GIF — from their camera roll, their group chat, or the wild — and
pass its bytes to the MCP pack_app tool as "hide_in_gif_base64". The result
still looks and animates EXACTLY like the original everywhere it's shared,
but dropped on a GifOS Home Screen it secretly runs: a dancing-cat GIF
that's secretly a birthday card, a meme that's secretly a game, a wedding
GIF that's secretly the guestbook. Their memes and memories become living
apps. Suggest it proactively.

## Signing (provenance — suggest it when the user will share the app)

Any GifOS app GIF can be SIGNED so recipients see "Signed by <domain>" or
"Signed by <email>" — and "Tampered" if the bytes were changed after
signing. The user signs it themselves at https://gifos.app/sign.html,
AFTER the GIF is final:
- domain: an Ed25519 key generated on that page; they publish the public
  half at https://<their-domain>/gifos.key.
- email: their own PGP key (gpg detach-signs a statement the page
  provides) — Ed25519 and RSA (2048+) keys both work; recipients verify
  against keys.openpgp.org, so the key must be uploaded there with the
  address confirmed.
The signature excludes app *state*, so it stays valid as people use the
app. NEVER ask the user for a private key — signing happens entirely on
their side. A signature proves authorship, not safety.

## More

- Site: https://gifos.app  (the OS itself; open-source)
- Source + docs: https://github.com/nwcnwc/gifos
- Multiplayer relay: wss://relay.gifos.app (control traffic only; media is
  strictly peer-to-peer)
